source: IPSO ruling 06628-24, Moore v Daily Mail (headline not supported by text)
article: LABOUR'S 400 POUND TAX HIT TO YOUR FAMILY HOLIDAY. Families flying abroad next year face higher costs under the government's plans, which will push Air Passenger Duty for a family of four travelling to the United States to 408 pounds, up from 352 pounds.

critiques.text_quote: LABOUR'S 400 POUND TAX HIT TO YOUR FAMILY HOLIDAY
critiques.issue_label: headline not supported by the text
critiques.question: The headline pins 400 pounds on Labour. Your own body text says their increase is 56 pounds and the other 352 was already there. A reader who only sees the headline, which is most readers, walks away with a number that is off by seven times. What headline can the body of the story actually defend?
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO upheld an accuracy breach on this exact Daily Mail headline because the article existed to correct its own front page. When the contradiction is internal, critics do not even have to do the work.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Make the headline carry only the increase Labour is responsible for, not the cumulative total.
critiques.replacement: LABOUR'S 56 POUND RISE ON YOUR FAMILY HOLIDAY TAX

source: IPSO ruling 01710-24, Full Fact v Daily Express (estimate presented as fact)
article: The party's policy would see Britain take in 100,000 extra illegal migrants, already in Europe, every year.

critiques.text_quote: would see Britain take in 100,000 extra illegal migrants, already in Europe, every year
critiques.issue_label: an estimate presented as established fact
critiques.question: Where does 100,000 come from? If this is one party's projection, built on an assumption about what a future government would do, then it is conjecture, and we have written it as plain fact with no 'the opposition estimates' anywhere near it.
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO upheld a breach against the Daily Express for presenting a Conservative Party estimate as fact, even inside a comment piece. A figure with a hidden author and a hidden assumption is the easiest thing for the other side to pull apart.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Attribute the figure to whoever produced it and state the assumption it rests on, so the reader can weigh it.
critiques.replacement: would, the opposition estimates, see Britain take in 100,000 extra migrants a year, a figure that assumes Britain joins an EU relocation scheme

source: IPSO ruling 00498-15, Black v Sunday Express (headline contradicted by the article body)
article: PRISONERS 'VIRTUALLY ROAMING AT WILL'. New privacy locks fitted to cell doors have handed inmates including Ian Huntley and Rose West more control over their day, though prison officers retain master keys that override the locks at any time.

critiques.text_quote: PRISONERS 'VIRTUALLY ROAMING AT WILL'
critiques.question: The headline says inmates are roaming at will. Your own third clause says officers hold override keys. The story disproves its own headline. Which one do we stand behind?
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO upheld a breach on this Sunday Express headline because it wrongly suggested the locks gave prisoners freedom of movement, when the article itself explained they did not. A headline the text contradicts is an unforced error a critic will screenshot.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Write the headline the body supports: the locks changed privacy over belongings, not freedom of movement.
critiques.replacement: NEW CELL PRIVACY LOCKS GIVE INMATES MORE CONTROL OVER BELONGINGS

source: IPSO rulings on Daily Express headlines, 2024 (causal claim the figures do not support)
article: Rachel Reeves sends mortgage rates soaring to highest in 18 months after Labour budget, with five-year fixed deals climbing through December.

critiques.text_quote: sends mortgage rates soaring to highest in 18 months after Labour budget
critiques.issue_label: causal claim the figures do not support
critiques.question: Highest in 18 months? Five-year fixed rates were higher in June than they were in December. Before we say the budget 'sends' rates to a record, have we actually checked the full 18-month series?
critiques.why_it_matters: IPSO upheld a breach on a Daily Express headline making this exact claim, because the rates had in fact been higher months earlier. A headline that asserts a record and a cause, when the data shows neither, hands critics the whole story.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Drop the record claim and the causal verb unless the series supports both. Report the actual movement and let readers see it.
critiques.replacement: Mortgage rates rise after the budget, though they remain below their June peak

source: Australian Press Council adjudication (repeated 'election fixer' label implying involvement)
article: The election fixer at the centre of the scandal was photographed leaving the building on Tuesday. The election fixer has not been charged with any offence.

critiques.text_quote: The election fixer
critiques.issue_label: a loaded label repeated as proven
critiques.question: We call him 'the election fixer' twice, in our own voice, in a story that also says he has not been charged with anything. The label does the accusing for us. Has a court or an inquiry actually established that he fixed an election?
critiques.why_it_matters: The Australian Press Council upheld a complaint over repeated, prominent references to an 'election fixer' that unfairly implied involvement. A label we have not proven is the line the subject's defenders quote first.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use a neutral identifier and let the established facts carry the weight. If the label is justified, attribute it to the body that made the finding.
critiques.replacement: The consultant at the centre of the inquiry

source: Australian Press Council adjudication (statistics framed to imply a group is blameworthy)
article: A new report lists same-sex attraction alongside obesity, smoking and drug use as factors associated with poorer health outcomes.

critiques.text_quote: lists same-sex attraction alongside obesity, smoking and drug use
critiques.question: Putting same-sex attraction in a list with smoking and drug use does an argument the report may not make. The grouping itself tells the reader these things belong together. Does the underlying study actually treat them as comparable, or is the list our editing choice?
critiques.why_it_matters: The Australian Press Council upheld a complaint over a presentation that suggested same-sex attraction was unhealthy and blameworthy by listing it with behaviours like obesity and drug use. The framing carried a judgment the data did not.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Report each finding on its own terms. If the study groups them, say how and why; if we grouped them, ungroup them.

source: Press impartiality practice; the reporter's voice carries facts, not verdicts
article: The administration unveiled an aggressive new immigration enforcement policy on Wednesday that lets federal agents detain people at courthouses and hospitals.

critiques.text_quote: an aggressive new immigration enforcement policy
critiques.question: 'Aggressive' is our judgment, not a fact about the policy. To a hostile reader that one word is proof we picked a side, and they will use it to wave away everything factual that follows.
critiques.why_it_matters: Impartiality practice across the press codes is consistent: the reporter's voice should carry the facts, not the verdict. A loaded adjective we did not need is an unforced error.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use a neutral descriptor that reports the same scope, or attribute 'aggressive' to a named critic.
critiques.replacement: an expansive new immigration enforcement policy

source: Fairness practice on placement of the subject's response
article: The mayor accepted more than 40,000 dollars in travel and lodging from a developer who held a major city contract, records show. (Twelve paragraphs later.) In a statement, the mayor's office said the gifts were fully disclosed and the contract was awarded by competitive bid years before any of the trips.

critiques.text_quote: (Twelve paragraphs later.) In a statement, the mayor's office said the gifts were fully disclosed and the contract was awarded by competitive bid years before any of the trips.
critiques.question: The mayor's actual answer, that the gifts were disclosed and the contract predates the trips, is twelve paragraphs down. By then most readers are gone and every share is just the first line. Why is the response not near the top?
critiques.why_it_matters: When the rebuttal lives below the fold, the subject's defenders can credibly say it was hidden, and the story becomes about our fairness instead of the conduct. Moving it up costs us nothing.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Bring the core of the response into the first few paragraphs. One sentence is enough to inoculate the piece, then continue the reporting.

source: Fairness practice on guilt by association
article: Senator Hayes voted against the consumer bill on Tuesday. Last month he was seen at a Washington steakhouse with industrialist Roy Marsh, who has lobbied against the bill.

critiques.text_quote: Last month he was seen at a Washington steakhouse with industrialist Roy Marsh, who has lobbied against the bill.
critiques.issue_label: guilt by association without a link
critiques.question: We have placed Hayes in a steakhouse with Marsh and trusted the reader to draw the line for us. Did they discuss the bill? Did Marsh fund Hayes? If we can prove a functional link, that is the story. If we cannot, the steakhouse is innuendo.
critiques.why_it_matters: A proximity detail with no established link is exactly the line a hostile critic pulls to argue the whole piece is built on insinuation. It is the weakest brick and it is load-bearing.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Establish the actual connection, donations, lobbying records, meetings on the bill, or cut the steakhouse line.

source: Fairness practice on symmetrical treatment of named parties
article: The soft-spoken teacher, who has devoted twenty years to the district, filed the complaint. The administrator she accused, a former corporate lawyer, declined to comment.

critiques.text_quote: The soft-spoken teacher, who has devoted twenty years to the district, filed the complaint. The administrator she accused, a former corporate lawyer, declined to comment.
critiques.question: One party gets 'soft-spoken' and 'devoted twenty years'. The other gets 'former corporate lawyer'. Each phrase may be accurate, but side by side they tell the reader who to trust before a single fact is in. Is there a reporting reason for the asymmetry?
critiques.why_it_matters: Readers register the asymmetry even when every individual phrase checks out. It is the pattern a critic points to, and it is avoidable.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Describe both parties at the same depth and in the same register, or drop the colour from both until the facts have done their work.

source: Fairness practice on photo and caption juxtaposition
article: Caption: Senator Voss smiles during yesterday's hearing on the contaminated water settlement, in which families described decades of illness.

critiques.text_quote: Senator Voss smiles during yesterday's hearing on the contaminated water settlement, in which families described decades of illness
critiques.issue_label: image and caption pinning a mood to a moment
critiques.question: We have chosen a frame of him smiling and put it next to families describing illness. He may have been smiling at a colleague's joke between segments. Are we accurately describing what was happening, or are we choosing a frame and a caption that imply contempt?
critiques.why_it_matters: Hostile critics will compare our caption to the unedited video and say we cherry-picked the most damaging single frame. The same accurate-on-its-face caption can mislead about the moment.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Choose an image that represents the typical demeanour of the hearing or remove the colour from the caption. If the smile is genuinely the story, sourcing has to do that work, not the image.

source: Fairness practice on pull-quote sharpening
article: Pull quote (in 36-point type next to her photograph): 'I hate this neighbourhood.' Body text: 'Some days I hate this neighbourhood, but it's also where my mother is buried and where my kids grew up.'

critiques.text_quote: Pull quote (in 36-point type next to her photograph): 'I hate this neighbourhood.'
critiques.issue_label: pull quote sharpens past the source's meaning
critiques.question: She said 'some days I hate this neighbourhood, but it's also where my mother is buried.' The pull quote keeps the heat and drops the love. What followers and Google will see is the four-word version, not the full sentence. Is the four-word version what she said?
critiques.why_it_matters: Pull quotes that strip qualifier clauses are how subjects feel betrayed by accurate reporting. Critics will compare the pull quote to the body and call it manipulation, even when each phrase is real.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Choose a pull quote that preserves the meaning. 'Some days I hate it, but it's home' carries the same heat without losing the second half.
critiques.replacement: 'Some days I hate this neighbourhood, but it's also home.'

source: Fairness practice on 'refused to comment'
article: Reached at his office Friday, Mr Patel refused to comment on the allegations.

critiques.text_quote: refused to comment
critiques.question: Did he refuse, or did he decline through counsel, or did he say he would respond after the hearing? 'Refused' frames silence as defiance. The choice of verb does work the reporting may not support.
critiques.why_it_matters: 'Refused' is one of the small verbs that read as guilty to a reader. Press-fairness practice consistently distinguishes refusal (hostile) from decline (neutral), and using the harsher word inaccurately is a recognised unforced error.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use the verb that fits the actual exchange. 'Declined to comment' is the default. 'Did not respond' if no response came back at all. 'Refused' only if he refused.

source: Fairness practice on question-mark headlines (Betteridge's law and the Daily Express tradition)
article: DID THE MAYOR KNOWINGLY MISLEAD THE COUNCIL ABOUT THE BUDGET? Hours after an internal review found accounting errors, sources say...

critiques.text_quote: DID THE MAYOR KNOWINGLY MISLEAD THE COUNCIL ABOUT THE BUDGET?
critiques.issue_label: question-mark headline pinning an unproven claim
critiques.question: A question-mark headline pretends to ask, but it plants the accusation. The reader reads 'mayor knowingly misled' before they read 'maybe not'. If we cannot say it as a statement, can we say it as a question?
critiques.why_it_matters: Press regulators have repeatedly upheld complaints over question-mark headlines that imply allegations the body cannot support. The technique is so well known it has a name and is used against newsrooms in court.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Either prove the claim and headline it as a statement, or rewrite the question so it does not embed the accusation: 'The mayor faces questions over the budget review.'
critiques.replacement: MAYOR FACES QUESTIONS AFTER COUNCIL BUDGET REVIEW

source: Fairness practice on 'critics say' without naming any critics
article: Critics say the policy will hurt small businesses and chase investment out of the state.

critiques.text_quote: Critics say
critiques.question: Which critics? Two names would do it. 'Critics say' is the construction we reach for when we have not actually called anyone, or when the only critics we found were so partisan we did not want to name them. Either way the line carries a claim with no one behind it.
critiques.why_it_matters: An unnamed-critic line is a tell. A subject's defenders read it as 'the reporter could not find a real critic so they made one up', and they are sometimes right. It is the easiest sentence in the story to attack.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Name two specific critics with their reasoning, or cut the line. If only partisan voices oppose the policy, attribute it to them by name and let the reader weigh it.
critiques.replacement: The state's chamber of commerce and the small business association said the policy would chase investment out of the state

source: Fairness practice on selective timing in the lede
article: Just three days after meeting privately with the developer, Patel approved the variance.

critiques.text_quote: Just three days after meeting privately with the developer
critiques.question: 'Just three days after' is a strong claim about cause. Variances often take months to schedule and the meeting could have been on a different topic entirely. Do we have evidence the meeting drove the approval, or only that the two events were close in time?
critiques.why_it_matters: Selective temporal framing is one of the most common ways stories overclaim. A neutral reader reads cause; a hostile reader reads confirmation of bias on our part.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Report what we actually know about the meeting and the approval timeline. If we have evidence the meeting moved the approval, lead with that evidence, not with the calendar.
critiques.replacement: Patel met privately with the developer three days before approving the variance, which had been pending review for four months

source: Fairness practice on stacking past-conduct labels
article: The controversial, embattled, scandal-hit mayor faces another inquiry this week.

critiques.text_quote: The controversial, embattled, scandal-hit mayor
critiques.question: Three loaded adjectives in one phrase. Each one does the work of saying she has done something wrong, without us having to point to a specific finding. Has anything been found by an inquiry, or are we relying on the cumulative weight of words?
critiques.why_it_matters: Stacking adjectives is the rhetorical equivalent of running the same accusation three times. Each one is hard to defend on its own; together they tell the reader to assume the worst.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use the specific record instead: 'The mayor, who faced ethics findings on two of the three previous inquiries.' If the specifics do not justify the heat, drop the adjectives.
critiques.replacement: The mayor, who has been the subject of two prior ethics findings,

source: Fairness practice on framing a routine action as hostile
article: The senator rushed the bill through the committee on Friday afternoon, snuck it past objections, and forced a vote before opponents could rally.

critiques.text_quote: rushed the bill through the committee on Friday afternoon, snuck it past objections, and forced a vote
critiques.issue_label: hostile verbs on a routine procedure
critiques.question: Three loaded verbs (rushed, snuck, forced) on three procedural steps that committees do every week. Was the schedule actually unusual, were the objections actually substantive, was the vote actually compressed? Or are we colouring routine procedure?
critiques.why_it_matters: Hostile-verb stacking is the surest way to telegraph a position to the reader. Critics will compare our verbs to neutral coverage of the same procedure and the contrast will be the story.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use neutral verbs and report the timeline. If the schedule was genuinely unusual, show how compared to similar bills, and let the reader judge.
critiques.replacement: moved the bill through committee on Friday afternoon and called a vote later that day, despite objections from two members

source: Fairness practice on selective quoting of an opposing voice
article: Asked about the funding cut, Councilwoman Reyes said, 'You can't possibly justify spending more right now. I mean, look at the deficit. We have no choice.' We have no choice, Reyes told the council.

critiques.text_quote: We have no choice, Reyes told the council.
critiques.question: She said 'You can't justify spending more, we have no choice.' The trailing quote we lifted out, 'We have no choice', reads as if she felt forced into the cut by something other than her own argument. Is the lifted quote what she meant?
critiques.why_it_matters: Quoting half a sentence to flip its meaning is the textbook fairness failure. Press councils worldwide have upheld complaints on this exact move.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Quote the full clause or summarise her position accurately. If the four-word lift is what we want to highlight, anchor it to the full quote so the reader can see the context.
critiques.replacement: 'We have no choice but to cut,' Reyes told the council, citing the deficit

source: Fairness practice on choosing the most extreme image
article: Above the article: a photograph from the 2018 protest, the only one in the file where a window broke.

critiques.text_quote: Above the article: a photograph from the 2018 protest, the only one in the file where a window broke.
critiques.question: We have the wire archive's full set from that day. We chose the one frame with a broken window. Is the article actually about that incident, or are we using the most dramatic single image to colour a piece that is mostly about something else?
critiques.why_it_matters: Image selection that diverges sharply from the article's content is a recognised fairness failure. Defenders of the protest will produce the rest of the archive and the contrast becomes the story about us.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use a representative image, or write the caption to anchor the frame to the specific incident the picture shows.

source: Fairness practice on smearing by past record
article: Marcus Reynolds, a former state senator who resigned in 2018 after federal prosecutors charged him with wire fraud, has been hired as a consultant by Governor Mitchell.

source: Example of a neutral, well-balanced passage
article: The county will raise the property tax rate by 4 percent next year, the commission said Tuesday. The increase, the first since 2019, will add about 120 dollars to the annual bill on a median-priced home.

source: Example of a loaded adjective correctly attributed to its source
article: Critics called the policy 'aggressive' and said it went too far. The governor's office defended it as 'a measured response to a real problem.'

source: Example of a routine procedure described neutrally
article: The senate health committee voted 7 to 4 on Tuesday to advance the bill to the floor, after a one-hour markup. Two committee members said they would file dissenting reports.
